subject: Tort Law
lesson_title: The Reasonable Foreseeability Test, Kind vs Manner, and the Egg-Shell Skull Rule

questions.question: How does remoteness of damage differ from factual causation in negligence?

options.A: Causation asks whether the act brought about the harm; remoteness asks whether that type of harm should be attributed to the defendant
options.B: Causation concerns the defendant's duty; remoteness concerns the standard of care
options.C: Remoteness and causation are the same inquiry approached from different angles
options.D: Causation concerns the plaintiff's loss; remoteness concerns the defendant's intent

questions.explanation: These are separate inquiries: causation (factual) asks whether the defendant's act brought about the harm; remoteness asks whether that harm is of a type that should, in fairness, be attributed to the defendant. Conflating them is a common FE-1 exam error.

questions.question: Which case established the directness test for remoteness of damage?

options.A: Wagon Mound No 1 [1961]
options.B: Re Polemis and Furness, Withy and Company [1921]
options.C: Hughes v Lord Advocate [1963]
options.D: Smith v Leech Brain [1962]

questions.explanation: Re Polemis and Furness, Withy and Company [1921] established the directness test: the defendant is liable for all harm that is a direct consequence of their negligent act, regardless of whether it was foreseeable. This test was later rejected by Wagon Mound No 1.

questions.question: Under the directness test from Re Polemis, a defendant is liable for harm that is:

options.A: Both foreseeable in type and direct in causation
options.B: Foreseeable as a reasonable consequence of the negligent act
options.C: A direct consequence of their negligence, regardless of whether it was foreseeable
options.D: Of the same kind as the foreseeable harm, even if the manner differs

questions.explanation: Under the Re Polemis directness test, foreseeability of the specific harm was irrelevant — what mattered was directness. If the harm was a direct consequence of the negligent act, the defendant was fully liable, however unforeseeable the extent or type of harm.

questions.question: In Re Polemis, the harm that led to the ship's destruction was:

options.A: Oil ignited by welding, causing fire to spread across water
options.B: A paraffin lamp knocked into a hold triggering a gas explosion
options.C: An unexpected chemical reaction between dropped cargo and the ship's fuel
options.D: A spark from a negligently dropped plank, which ignited petrol vapour in the ship's hold

questions.explanation: In Re Polemis, a plank was negligently dropped into the ship's hold. The plank caused a spark, which ignited petrol vapour, destroying the entire ship. The Court of Appeal held the defendants liable for the total destruction as it was a direct consequence of dropping the plank.

questions.question: Which court and case rejected the directness test and replaced it with the reasonable foreseeability test?

options.A: The Privy Council in Overseas Tankship (UK) Ltd v Morts Dock and Engineering Co [1961]
options.B: The House of Lords in Hughes v Lord Advocate [1963]
options.C: The Irish Supreme Court in Glencar Exploration plc v Mayo County Council [2002]
options.D: The Court of Appeal in Doughty v Turner Manufacturing Co [1964]

questions.explanation: The Privy Council in Overseas Tankship (UK) Ltd v Morts Dock and Engineering Co [1961] — universally known as Wagon Mound No 1 — outright rejected the Re Polemis directness test. Lord Simonds held that defendants should only be liable for foreseeable types of damage.

questions.question: The case Overseas Tankship (UK) Ltd v Morts Dock and Engineering Co [1961] is universally known as:

options.A: Re Polemis
options.B: Wagon Mound No 1
options.C: Hughes v Lord Advocate
options.D: Doughty v Turner

questions.explanation: Overseas Tankship (UK) Ltd v Morts Dock and Engineering Co [1961] is universally referred to as Wagon Mound No 1, named after the ship involved. It is the leading authority on remoteness of damage in negligence in both English and Irish law.

questions.question: Under the Wagon Mound No 1 test, a defendant is liable for damage that:

options.A: Was a direct consequence of their negligent act
options.B: Was foreseeable both in type and in precise manner
options.C: Was of a type or kind reasonably foreseeable as a consequence of the negligent act
options.D: Arose from the same mechanism as the foreseeable harm

questions.explanation: The Wagon Mound No 1 test asks whether the damage was of a type or kind that a reasonable person would have foreseen as a consequence of the negligence. The precise manner or mechanism need not be foreseeable — only the type or kind of damage matters.

questions.question: In Wagon Mound No 1, why did the Privy Council find no liability for the fire damage to the wharf?

options.A: The defendants had not been negligent in spilling the oil
options.B: The oil spill had not factually caused the fire at the wharf
options.C: The plaintiff had assumed the risk of fire damage
options.D: Destruction of the wharf by fire was not a reasonably foreseeable consequence of spilling furnace oil on water in those circumstances

questions.explanation: In Wagon Mound No 1, the defendants' employees had been advised the oil would not ignite on water, and welding continued. The Privy Council held that destruction of the wharf by fire was not a reasonably foreseeable consequence of spilling furnace oil on water in those circumstances — therefore no liability for the fire damage.

questions.question: In Wagon Mound No 1, what advice did the defendants' employees receive about the spilled oil?

options.A: The oil would not ignite on water
options.B: The oil posed a foreseeable fire risk and welding operations should cease
options.C: The oil would disperse harmlessly within 24 hours
options.D: The oil could ignite if exposed to high heat from welding

questions.explanation: In Wagon Mound No 1, the defendants sought expert advice and were told the oil would not ignite on water. On that basis, welding continued. Two days later, a piece of molten metal ignited cotton waste floating in the oil, setting the oil alight and destroying the wharf.

questions.question: The 'Wagon Mound No 1 test' and the 'reasonable foreseeability test' for remoteness are:

options.A: Different tests applying to different types of harm
options.B: Two names for the same governing test for remoteness in Irish and English tort law
options.C: Distinct in that Wagon Mound applies in England while reasonable foreseeability applies in Ireland
options.D: Related but distinct: Wagon Mound deals with property damage; reasonable foreseeability with personal injury

questions.explanation: The Wagon Mound No 1 test is the reasonable foreseeability test — they are two names for the same thing. The case established that defendants are only liable for damage of a type or kind a reasonable person would have foreseen. Both names may be used in FE-1 answers.

questions.question: In Hughes v Lord Advocate [1963], what caused the plaintiff's burns?

options.A: A chemical eruption from asbestos cement dropped into a cauldron
options.B: A splash of molten metal from a negligently operated lathe
options.C: An explosion caused when a paraffin warning lamp was knocked into a manhole
options.D: Furnace oil ignited by welding operations at a wharf

questions.explanation: In Hughes v Lord Advocate, Post Office workers left a manhole with paraffin warning lamps and went for a tea break. Two boys played around the shelter; one knocked a paraffin lamp into the manhole. A violent explosion followed, the plaintiff fell into the manhole and suffered severe burns.

questions.question: The defendants in Hughes v Lord Advocate argued that liability should be denied because:

options.A: They had not been negligent in leaving the paraffin lamps unattended
options.B: The plaintiff was a child trespasser who had assumed the risk
options.C: Burns in general were not a foreseeable type of harm
options.D: An explosion was a different kind of event from anything that had been foreseeable

questions.explanation: The defendants in Hughes v Lord Advocate argued that the explosion — caused by unexpected vapourisation of paraffin — was a different kind of event from anything foreseeable. They accepted burns from a lamp were foreseeable, but argued the explosion represented a wholly different type of occurrence.

questions.question: The House of Lords held in Hughes v Lord Advocate that the defendants were liable because:

options.A: The kind of damage — burns from fire — was foreseeable, and the unforeseeable mechanism of explosion did not defeat liability
options.B: The explosion itself was foreseeable given that paraffin lamps were left unattended
options.C: The plaintiff was a child and a higher duty of care was owed
options.D: The directness test from Re Polemis applied and the explosion was a direct consequence

questions.explanation: The House of Lords in Hughes v Lord Advocate asked what kind of damage was foreseeable — burns from fire. An explosion causing burns was still the same type of harm: fire damage causing burns. The fact that the precise mechanism (vapourisation and explosion) was unforeseeable did not matter — the kind of damage was foreseeable.

questions.question: What principle does Hughes v Lord Advocate establish for the type/manner distinction?

options.A: A different mechanism producing the same type of harm defeats liability where the mechanism itself was not foreseeable
options.B: Where the type or kind of damage was foreseeable, the defendant cannot escape liability merely because the manner in which it occurred was unexpected
options.C: The defendant is liable for all consequences once any element of negligence is established
options.D: Foreseeability of mechanism is required in addition to foreseeability of type

questions.explanation: Hughes v Lord Advocate establishes that provided the type or kind of damage was foreseeable, the defendant cannot escape liability merely because the manner in which it occurred was unexpected. The analysis focuses on the type of harm, not the precise route by which it came about.

questions.question: In Doughty v Turner Manufacturing Co [1964], what caused the plaintiff's injuries?

options.A: A splash of molten liquid from the accidental dropping of an asbestos cover
options.B: Burns from a fire caused by a paraffin lamp knocked into a pit of chemicals
options.C: An unexpected chemical eruption triggered when asbestos cement reacted with extremely hot liquid compound at 800 degrees Celsius
options.D: An explosion from petrol vapour ignited by a spark from a dropped tool

questions.explanation: In Doughty v Turner Manufacturing, an asbestos cement cover was accidentally knocked into a cauldron of extremely hot liquid compound at 800 degrees Celsius. Rather than causing a splash, the asbestos cement underwent an unexpected chemical reaction, causing an eruption that scalded the plaintiff.

questions.question: The Court of Appeal denied liability in Doughty v Turner Manufacturing because:

options.A: The defendant had not been negligent in allowing the asbestos cover to fall
options.B: The plaintiff had contributed to his own injuries by standing too close
options.C: Burns from splashing were foreseeable but the plaintiff failed to prove causation
options.D: The chemical eruption was a fundamentally different kind of occurrence from the foreseeable splash — not merely a different mechanism producing the same type of harm

questions.explanation: In Doughty v Turner, burns from a splash were foreseeable. But the Court of Appeal held that the chemical eruption caused by the unexpected molecular reaction was a fundamentally different kind of physical event — not simply an unforeseeable route to the same type of harm as a splash. This distinguishes it from Hughes v Lord Advocate.

questions.question: Which analysis correctly reconciles Hughes v Lord Advocate and Doughty v Turner Manufacturing?

options.A: In Hughes, a different mechanism produced the same type of harm (burns from fire) — liability; in Doughty, the mechanism produced a fundamentally different kind of harm — no liability
options.B: In Hughes the mechanism was foreseeable; in Doughty it was not — hence the different outcomes
options.C: Both cases turned on whether the plaintiff was a foreseeable type of victim
options.D: Hughes applied the directness test; Doughty applied the Wagon Mound foreseeability test

questions.explanation: The key to reconciling these cases is the type/manner distinction. In Hughes, the explosion was an unexpected mechanism producing the same type of harm (fire causing burns) — liability followed. In Doughty, the chemical eruption was not simply a different route to the same harm but a fundamentally different physical event — liability was denied.

questions.question: The egg-shell skull rule is also known as:

options.A: The directness test
options.B: The thin skull rule
options.C: The kind versus manner rule
options.D: The assumption of risk rule

questions.explanation: The egg-shell skull rule is also known as the thin skull rule. Both names convey the same concept: a plaintiff with unusual vulnerability (a skull as fragile as an egg) must be compensated for the full extent of their injury by the tortfeasor, even if that extent was wholly unforeseeable.

questions.question: The egg-shell skull rule provides that:

options.A: A defendant is only liable for the harm a person of ordinary fortitude would have suffered
options.B: The plaintiff must disclose pre-existing conditions before the rule applies
options.C: Once a type of injury is foreseeable, the defendant must take the plaintiff as they find them and is liable for the full extent of harm
options.D: The rule applies only where the defendant knew of the plaintiff's vulnerability

questions.explanation: The egg-shell skull rule provides that once a type of injury is foreseeable, the defendant must take the plaintiff as they find them. The defendant is liable for the full extent of the plaintiff's injuries — even if that extent was wholly unforeseeable due to a pre-existing vulnerability — not just for what a person of ordinary fortitude would have suffered.

questions.question: The leading case establishing the egg-shell skull rule is:

options.A: Hughes v Lord Advocate [1963]
options.B: Wagon Mound No 1 [1961]
options.C: Re Polemis [1921]
options.D: Smith v Leech Brain and Company Limited [1962]

questions.explanation: Smith v Leech Brain and Company Limited [1962] is the leading case on the egg-shell skull rule. Lord Parker held that once some physical injury from burning was foreseeable, the defendants were liable for all consequences — including the unforeseeable development of cancer triggered by the plaintiff's pre-existing pre-cancerous condition.

questions.question: In Smith v Leech Brain [1962], what was the pre-existing condition of the plaintiff?

options.A: A pre-cancerous condition that was triggered into full cancer by a burn to the lip from molten metal
options.B: A pre-existing skull fragility that caused catastrophic fracture from a minor head injury
options.C: A pre-existing psychiatric condition that was worsened by a negligently caused shock
options.D: A pre-existing eye condition that caused blindness from what would otherwise be a minor injury

questions.explanation: In Smith v Leech Brain, a workman's lip was burned by a splash of molten metal — a foreseeable type of injury. The plaintiff had a pre-existing pre-cancerous condition, which the burn triggered into full-blown cancer. He died as a result. Lord Parker held the defendants liable for all consequences.

questions.question: Lord Parker's holding in Smith v Leech Brain established that:

options.A: The defendants were not liable because cancer and death were entirely unforeseeable
options.B: Once some physical injury from burning was foreseeable, the defendants were liable for all consequences including the unforeseeable development of cancer
options.C: The defendants were liable only for the burn itself, not the consequential cancer
options.D: The egg-shell skull rule applies only to injuries to the skull or head

questions.explanation: Lord Parker held in Smith v Leech Brain that once some physical injury from burning was foreseeable, the defendants were liable for all the consequences of that burning — including the wholly unforeseeable development of cancer caused by the plaintiff's pre-existing pre-cancerous condition. This is the classic statement of the egg-shell skull rule.

questions.question: To which types of pre-existing vulnerability does the egg-shell skull rule apply?

options.A: Physical vulnerabilities only
options.B: Physical and psychiatric vulnerabilities only
options.C: Physical, financial, and psychological vulnerabilities
options.D: Financial vulnerabilities only, since they cannot be detected in advance

questions.explanation: The egg-shell skull rule applies to all types of pre-existing vulnerability: physical (e.g., pre-cancerous conditions), financial (e.g., unusual earnings capacity), and psychological (e.g., pre-existing psychiatric conditions). In all cases, if a type of harm was foreseeable, the defendant takes the plaintiff as they find them.

questions.question: A plaintiff with an unusual and highly lucrative occupation suffers a broken arm due to the defendant's negligence. Because of the plaintiff's occupation, loss of earnings is far greater than it would be for an ordinary person. What principle governs the recoverable loss?

options.A: The Wagon Mound test — the defendant is only liable for foreseeable earnings loss
options.B: Re Polemis — all direct consequences are recoverable
options.C: Hughes v Lord Advocate — the manner of loss was foreseeable
options.D: The egg-shell skull rule — the defendant takes the plaintiff as they find them, including their financial circumstances

questions.explanation: The egg-shell skull rule applies to financial vulnerabilities as well as physical and psychological ones. Once personal injury was a foreseeable type of harm, the defendant must take the plaintiff as they find them — including their unusual and lucrative occupation — and is liable for the full, disproportionate financial loss.

questions.question: What precondition must be satisfied before the egg-shell skull rule is engaged?

options.A: Some type or kind of injury must have been reasonably foreseeable
options.B: The defendant must have had actual knowledge of the plaintiff's pre-existing vulnerability
options.C: The pre-existing condition must have been medically documented before the tort occurred
options.D: The full extent of the harm must have been a foreseeable consequence of the negligence

questions.explanation: The egg-shell skull rule operates as a gloss on the Wagon Mound test. Before the rule is engaged, some type or kind of injury must have been reasonably foreseeable — satisfying the Wagon Mound threshold. Once that is established, the defendant is liable for the full extent regardless of the plaintiff's pre-existing vulnerability.

questions.question: A defendant negligently causes a minor car collision. The plaintiff has a pre-existing back condition and suffers far greater back injury than an ordinary person would. Assuming some back injury was foreseeable, which principle determines the defendant's liability for the full extent?

options.A: The directness test from Re Polemis
options.B: The egg-shell skull rule — the defendant takes the plaintiff as they find them
options.C: The Wagon Mound type/kind test — the full extent must itself be foreseeable
options.D: Hughes v Lord Advocate — the precise mechanism of aggravation was foreseeable

questions.explanation: Once back injury was foreseeable (the Wagon Mound threshold), the egg-shell skull rule applies. The defendant must take the plaintiff as they find them — including the pre-existing back condition — and is liable for the full extent of injury, even though it is far greater than what an ordinary person would have suffered.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why the egg-shell skull rule is described as a 'gloss' on the Wagon Mound test?

options.A: The egg-shell skull rule replaces the Wagon Mound test for personal injury cases
options.B: The egg-shell skull rule reverses the Wagon Mound test's requirement of foreseeability entirely
options.C: The egg-shell skull rule applies after the Wagon Mound foreseeability test is satisfied — once a type of harm is foreseeable, it extends liability to the full unforeseeable extent
options.D: The egg-shell skull rule operates independently of Wagon Mound and applies whenever a plaintiff has a pre-existing condition

questions.explanation: The egg-shell skull rule is a 'gloss' because it supplements the Wagon Mound test rather than replacing it. The Wagon Mound test must first be satisfied (a type of harm must be foreseeable). Once it is, the egg-shell skull rule extends liability to the full extent of harm caused, even where that extent was wholly unforeseeable due to the plaintiff's particular vulnerability.

questions.question: In an FE-1 problem where a plaintiff is harmed in a surprising way by a defendant's negligence, which is the correct analytical approach to remoteness?

options.A: Apply Re Polemis first; if the harm is direct, impose liability without considering foreseeability
options.B: Apply the egg-shell skull rule first, then Wagon Mound if the plaintiff is vulnerable
options.C: Ask only whether causation is established; remoteness need not be addressed separately
options.D: Apply Wagon Mound — was the type of harm foreseeable? Then Hughes/Doughty — same type via unexpected mechanism? Then egg-shell skull if pre-existing vulnerability exists

questions.explanation: The correct FE-1 approach to remoteness: (1) Apply Wagon Mound — was the type or kind of damage reasonably foreseeable? (2) If harm was surprising in manner, apply Hughes v Lord Advocate / Doughty analysis — same type via unexpected mechanism, or fundamentally different type? (3) If plaintiff has pre-existing vulnerability, apply egg-shell skull rule. Re Polemis is no longer the governing test.

questions.question: A plaintiff is burned when a defendant negligently leaves a heat source unattended. Due to a rare skin condition, the burn triggers widespread infection and the plaintiff loses a limb. Assuming burns were a foreseeable type of harm, which analysis is correct?

options.A: The egg-shell skull rule applies — the defendant is liable for the full extent including the infection and amputation
options.B: Wagon Mound applies — amputation was not a foreseeable type of harm, so no liability for it
options.C: Hughes v Lord Advocate applies — the infection was an unexpected manner of the foreseeable burn harm
options.D: Doughty v Turner applies — the infection was a fundamentally different kind of harm from a burn

questions.explanation: Once burns were a foreseeable type of harm, the egg-shell skull rule applies. The plaintiff's rare skin condition making them susceptible to infection and amputation is a pre-existing physical vulnerability. The defendant must take the plaintiff as they find them and is liable for the full extent — infection and loss of limb included.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the current position on remoteness in Irish tort law?

options.A: The directness test from Re Polemis applies in Ireland as modified by the Irish Constitution
options.B: The Wagon Mound No 1 reasonable foreseeability test governs, asking whether the type or kind of damage was foreseeable
options.C: Ireland applies both the directness test and reasonable foreseeability depending on whether damage is to property or person
options.D: Ireland has not adopted Wagon Mound and continues to apply Re Polemis

questions.explanation: The Wagon Mound No 1 reasonable foreseeability test is the governing test for remoteness in Ireland. The question is always whether the type or kind of damage was reasonably foreseeable as a consequence of the negligent act. Re Polemis has been rejected and is no longer applied.

questions.question: A fire negligently started reaches a factory containing chemicals, producing a large explosion and toxic gas release. The defendant argues only fire damage was foreseeable, not a chemical explosion. How should a court analyse this under remoteness?

options.A: Apply Re Polemis — the explosion and gas release were direct consequences of the fire
options.B: Apply Doughty v Turner — the chemical explosion was a fundamentally different kind of harm from fire
options.C: Apply Hughes v Lord Advocate — fire damage causing explosion and toxic release is the same type of harm (fire/chemical damage) via an unexpected mechanism; liability is likely
options.D: Apply the egg-shell skull rule — the factory's chemical contents are a pre-existing vulnerability

questions.explanation: This scenario calls for Hughes v Lord Advocate analysis. Was this a different mechanism producing the same type of harm (fire/chemical damage), or a fundamentally different kind of harm? Fire causing an explosion and toxic release is most naturally characterised as the same type of harm — fire and chemical damage — via an unexpected mechanism. Applying Hughes, liability is likely.

questions.question: Which proposition about remoteness is INCORRECT?

options.A: A defendant may be liable for damage of a foreseeable type even if the precise manner in which it occurred was unexpected
options.B: The egg-shell skull rule requires some foreseeable type of injury before it is engaged
options.C: Wagon Mound No 1 replaced the directness test from Re Polemis as the governing test for remoteness
options.D: Under the egg-shell skull rule, the defendant is only liable for the harm a person of ordinary fortitude would have suffered

questions.explanation: Option D is incorrect. The egg-shell skull rule holds the opposite: once a type of harm is foreseeable, the defendant is liable for the FULL extent of the plaintiff's injuries — not merely the harm a person of ordinary fortitude would have suffered. Options A (Hughes v Lord Advocate), B (Wagon Mound precondition), and C (Wagon Mound replacing Re Polemis) are all correct statements.
